mainEntity.name: How much does an OM642 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM642 typically costs £3,200–£5,500 supply only [MB-QUOTE], with fitting adding £650–£1,300 depending on labour rates. V6 complexity and specialist expertise drive costs above standard four-cylinder diesels.

mainEntity.name: Is the OM642 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 rates 3.5★ out of 5 — a strong, refined V6 diesel when maintained. Its defining weakness is the oil cooler seal, which fails predictably and requires significant labour to replace — but is not catastrophic if addressed promptly.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common OM642 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal failure (O-rings in the V of the engine) is the most common — it causes oil and coolant to mix, leading to overheating and potential engine damage. Swirl flap motor failure and turbo seal leaks are also well-documented issues.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes models use the OM642?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 powers a wide range — E‑Class W211/W212, ML‑Class W164, GL‑Class X164, S‑Class W221, CLS W219/W218, G‑Class W463, and Sprinter vans. It spans 2005 to present across multiple generations.

mainEntity.name: How long does the OM642 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With regular oil changes and prompt attention to oil cooler seals, 150,000–250,000 miles is realistic. Many well-maintained examples exceed 200,000 miles; the V6 is fundamentally durable with preventative care.

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz OM642 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes OM642 is a 3.0-litre V6 diesel — dependable long-term, but the oil cooler seal is its defining weak point. Backed by 880+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

verdictRating.confidence: 86% confidence — based on 880 UK enquiries, cross-referenced with specialist rebuild reports and manufacturer TSBs.

rows.score: 12 (Mix of cheap fixes and a few expensive, labour-intensive ones — oil cooler seal access requires significant disassembly)
16 (Well-supported via Mercedes dealer network and specialists, though some V6-specific parts carry a premium)
4 (Reconditioned cost as % of lowest host value — £4,350 on a £3,500–£4,000 car = 109–124% → 4)

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The OM642 is a strong, refined diesel V6 — but the oil cooler seal fails predictably and repairs are labour-intensive — insist on full service history."
verdictRating.bestFor: High-mileage motorway drivers who value V6 refinement and can budget for preventative maintenance.
verdictRating.avoidIf: Budget-conscious buyers shopping sub-£4k examples where a £1,500 oil cooler repair may not be economically justifiable.

rows.variantBadge: E 320 CDI / E 280 CDI / E 350 CDI
ML 320 CDI / ML 350 CDI / ML 280 CDI
GL 320 CDI / GL 350 CDI / GL 420 CDI

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an OM642 typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB‑QUOTE].
